answers.value: Proposed Lot 1: 3.50 acres (containing existing single-family dwelling, well, and septic); Proposed Lot 2: 1.75 acres (vacant land to remain undeveloped until future permitting). Acreages based on a boundary survey completed by Summit Peak Surveying, LLC, in May 2025.

answers.value:
We are requesting an exemption for certain illegal divisions of land in order to correct a historic, unpermitted split that occurred between a metes-and-bounds parcel (referred to in our deed as “Parcel A”) and an adjacent platted subdivision lot (Lot 7, Block 3, Pine Ridge Estates) in Clear Creek County.

Background and history:
When we purchased the property in August 2018, we believed we were buying a single, legally created parcel of approximately 5.25 acres, which included both the existing cabin and the adjacent open meadow area. Our purchase was based on a warranty deed recorded under Reception No. 201845679 and a marketing description from the listing agent that treated the property as one homesite. Only after beginning discussions with a local surveyor in late 2024 about building a detached garage and small studio space did we learn that, historically, the property was formed by combining a metes-and-bounds tract (Parcel A) with a separate platted lot (Lot 7 of Pine Ridge Estates) without going through a formal subdivision or exemption process.

Nature of the illegal division:
Based on conversations with Clear Creek County Planning staff and a review of older title documents, it appears that sometime in the late 1990s the then-owners informally adjusted boundaries and effectively merged portions of Parcel A and Lot 7 for private sale purposes. The configuration that resulted is not entirely consistent with the original subdivision plat nor with the legal description of the metes-and-bounds parcel. While subsequent deeds (including ours) attempted to describe the property as a single ownership, they did not resolve the underlying issue that the land was never properly legalized as two separate buildable lots or as a formally combined parcel.

Existing improvements and occupancy:
There is an existing two-bedroom single-family residence (originally constructed around 1982 and remodeled in 2005) located near what is now being surveyed as Proposed Lot 1. The residence has an on-site well (Permit No. 123456) and an individual septic system installed under a 2005 permit. However, we have been unable to locate clear documentation of a final Certificate of Occupancy from the County for the remodel and addition. Because of this lack of clear occupancy approval, and because the building sits in a location that does not clearly correspond to a legally created lot, we understand that the property currently falls under the category of “improved without proper County occupancy approval.”

Reason for seeking legalization now:
We are attempting to refinance our home in order to complete necessary safety and energy-efficiency upgrades, including roof replacement, electrical panel updates, and improved insulation. During the refinance process, both the title company and our lender raised concerns about the chain of title and the apparent irregular lot configuration. Our surveyor, Summit Peak Surveying, LLC, advised us that the cleanest long-term solution is to formally legalize the land division through the County’s exemption process, creating two clearly defined lots with accurate legal descriptions that reflect existing conditions on the ground.

We are not seeking to increase development density beyond what currently exists. Our primary goals are:
1. To ensure that the existing home is located on a single, clearly defined, legal lot (Proposed Lot 1) with adequate area for the existing well, septic system, driveway, and defensible space.
2. To create a second, vacant lot (Proposed Lot 2) that represents the remaining land and can either remain undeveloped under our ownership or be addressed in a future, fully compliant permitting process.
3. To satisfy the concerns of our lender and title company by having a legally recognized configuration that matches the survey and legal descriptions and avoids future confusion for us or any subsequent owners.

Public health, safety, and access considerations:
The existing driveway access from Evergreen Ridge Road will remain unchanged and serves the current residence safely, with adequate sight distance and emergency vehicle access as confirmed by Clear Creek Fire Authority during a 2023 wildfire mitigation assessment. Our septic system was inspected in 2021 by a licensed contractor and found to be in good working order, with recommended minor maintenance items that we have since completed. The well has been tested for basic potability parameters and continues to provide adequate domestic water for our family of four.

We understand that Proposed Lot 2, as a vacant parcel, will not be considered buildable without separate, future review and approval of access, utilities, on-site wastewater treatment, and all applicable zoning and building code requirements. We are comfortable with that limitation and are not asking the County to pre-approve any development on the second lot at this time. Our current focus is simply to correct the historical illegal division so that the County’s records, our survey, and our deed all align.

In summary, this exemption request is intended to:
- Rectify a long-standing, unintended illegal division of land created by prior owners without going through the County’s required processes.
- Provide clarity and certainty for both the County and our family regarding the legal status of our property.
- Ensure that any future improvements or ownership transfers can proceed on a sound legal and planning foundation.

We are fully willing to cooperate with any additional documentation, surveys, or conditions the Planning Department may require, and we appreciate the opportunity to bring this property into compliance with current County standards.
